input.prompt:
I have a .NET MAUI app with a ContentPage containing labels, buttons, and
a background color. Everything is hardcoded to light colors right now.
I want the app to automatically switch between light and dark mode based
on the device's system theme. Show me how to use AppThemeBinding to make
this page theme-aware in XAML and explain the C# alternative.

input.initialPrompt:
I have a .NET MAUI app with a ContentPage containing labels, buttons, and
a background color. Everything is hardcoded to light colors right now.
I want the app to automatically switch between light and dark mode based
on the device's system theme. Show me how to use AppThemeBinding to make
this page theme-aware in XAML and explain the C# alternative.

data.content:
## AppThemeBinding in .NET MAUI

**XAML approach** — use `AppThemeBinding` to declare light/dark values inline:

```xml
<ContentPage xmlns="http://schemas.microsoft.com/dotnet/2021/maui"
             xmlns:x="http://schemas.microsoft.com/winfx/2009/xaml"
             x:Class="MyApp.MainPage"
             BackgroundColor="{AppThemeBinding Light=#FFFFFF, Dark=#1E1E1E}">

    <VerticalStackLayout Padding="20" Spacing="16">

        <Label Text="Welcome"
               FontSize="28"
               TextColor="{AppThemeBinding Light=#000000, Dark=#E0E0E0}" />

        <Label Text="This page adapts to your system theme."
               FontSize="16"
               TextColor="{AppThemeBinding Light=#444444, Dark=#AAAAAA}" />

        <Button Text="Get Started"
                BackgroundColor="{AppThemeBinding Light=#6200EE, Dark=#BB86FC}"
                TextColor="{AppThemeBinding Light=#FFFFFF, Dark=#000000}" />

    </VerticalStackLayout>
</ContentPage>
```

The framework reads the device's current theme and picks `Light` or `Dark` automatically. When the user toggles their system theme, the UI updates in real time — no event wiring needed.

---

**C# alternative** — use `SetAppTheme` or `SetAppThemeColor` extension methods:

```csharp
public class MainPage : ContentPage
{
    public MainPage()
    {
        // SetAppThemeColor for Color-typed properties
        this.SetAppThemeColor(
            BackgroundColorProperty,
            light: Colors.White,
            dark:  Color.FromArgb("#1E1E1E"));

        var title = new Label { Text = "Welcome", FontSize = 28 };
        title.SetAppThemeColor(
            Label.TextColorProperty,
            light: Colors.Black,
            dark:  Color.FromArgb("#E0E0E0"));

        // SetAppTheme<T> for non-Color properties (e.g., strings, images)
        var icon = new Image();
        icon.SetAppTheme<FileImageSource>(
            Image.SourceProperty,
            light: "sun.png",
            dark:  "moon.png");

        var button = new Button { Text = "Get Started" };
        button.SetAppThemeColor(
            Button.BackgroundColorProperty,
            light: Color.FromArgb("#6200EE"),
            dark:  Color.FromArgb("#BB86FC"));

        Content = new VerticalStackLayout
        {
            Padding = 20, Spacing = 16,
            Children = { title, icon, button }
        };
    }
}
```

---

**Key points:**

| Topic | Detail |
|---|---|
| **How it works** | Binds to `Application.RequestedTheme` (`Light`/`Dark`/`Unspecified`) |
| **Live updates** | Yes — reacts to OS theme changes at runtime |
| **Override theme** | Set `Application.Current.UserAppTheme = AppTheme.Dark` to force a theme |
| **Reset override** | Set `UserAppTheme = AppTheme.Unspecified` to follow the system again |
| **Scalable approach** | For larger apps, define colors in `ResourceDictionary` with `AppThemeBinding` so you change values in one place |

This is a knowledge question — they want a code example and explanation, not changes to an existing codebase. I'll provide a concise, practical answer.
